quote: Has it always kinda been that high on you, or have you noticed it going up?
stewart_read: The rep captures Andrew's $200/month bill and attempts a follow-up question to deepen the emotional anchor — this is the right instinct. But Andrew's response ('Yeah. Please repeat, I don't understand') signals the question didn't land, and the rep immediately dropped it with 'Okay. Gotcha.' and pivoted to the pitch. The bill was captured but never flipped into alignment-against-the-utility. The codex's capture-and-flip pattern was available here and unused.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: the rep recognized Andrew had a language barrier and chose not to push the emotional anchor to avoid confusion. Rejected — the rep's pivot to the pitch ('we'll definitely see what we can do to get that a little bit lower') is generic, not a deliberate deferral to a later flip.
coaching_implication: Show the rep the codex's objection_inversion pattern. The $200/month number was in hand — the move was to flip it: 'That's exactly the kind of bill solar is designed to replace.' The rep had the data and didn't use it. In the 1-on-1, ask: 'What did you do with the $200 number after Andrew gave it to you?'

quote: And then on my end, I had here you were the homeowner. Was that right?
stewart_read: The rep confirms homeownership but never asks who else is on the title. This is a hard-required verify item per the codex — not because it's a checkbox, but because a co-owner excluded from the appointment is the primary structural reason appointments fall apart at the closer stage. The rep moved from homeowner confirmation directly to utility company without the co-owner question.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: the rep may have had co-owner data from the lead record and didn't need to ask. Rejected — there is no evidence in the transcript of any prior data reference, and the rep asks other verify items (roof type, utility) that would also be on a lead record.
coaching_implication: This is a direct no-show risk. If Andrew has a spouse or co-owner who isn't expecting a call tomorrow at 10AM, the sit evaporates. The rep gets no credit for this booking if Andrew no-shows. In the 1-on-1: 'After you confirmed he was the homeowner, what was the next question?' Walk the rep to the title/co-owner question as a non-negotiable follow-on.

quote: is just a good day to have one of our specialists come by and kinda run through the design with you
stewart_read: The rep initially frames the appointment as an in-person visit ('come by') before correcting to 'or even give you a phone call, whatever would be better for you.' This ambiguity is the likely source of Andrew's subsequent channel confusion — he pushes back on the appointment format and asks for email instead. The rep introduced the in-person framing first, which may have set an expectation the phone-call correction didn't fully clear.
alternative_hypothesis: Andrew's channel preference for email was pre-existing and unrelated to the 'come by' framing. Possible — but the sequencing (in-person framing → Andrew's pushback → email request) makes the causal link plausible enough to flag.
coaching_implication: The button_up protocol specifies phone appointment as the format. The rep should lead with phone, not offer in-person first and correct. In the 1-on-1: 'When you said come by, what did you mean? Walk me through how you set up the appointment format.' This is a script-discipline gap, not a judgment call.

quote: I need to I need to know your price and your sentence.
stewart_read: Andrew's second explicit price request is the clearest rebuttal opportunity in the call. The codex's price_estimate_before_design strategy calls for redirecting to the design process as the path to real numbers — not a generic 'a lot of information' deflection. The rep's response ('that's what we'll go over in the phone call') is procedurally correct but strategically inert — it tells Andrew to wait without giving him a reason to want to.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: the rep is deliberately holding price to preserve the closer's leverage. Partially valid — but the codex's redirect strategy creates curiosity rather than just deferring, and the rep's version creates no curiosity at all.
coaching_implication: Suggested rephrase (intent, not verbatim): 'Our specialist will pull that up in the first five minutes — they build it specific to your house and your ComEd usage, so the number is actually yours, not a ballpark.' This preserves the deferral while giving Andrew a reason to show up. In the 1-on-1: 'What did you say when he asked for the price? What were you trying to accomplish with that answer?'

quote: Alright. Well, I'll start on your design now, and we'll we'll get you down for tomorrow at 10AM.
stewart_read: The rep books the appointment but completes only one of the required button_up elements (appointment datetime confirmed). Missing: one-hour block stated, 'is that fair' handshake, no-driving/at-computer anchor, and text follow-up promise. Each missing element is a documented no-show risk factor. The booking is real but the confirmation protocol that makes it stick was not executed.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: the rep kept the close short because Andrew's language suggested he was near his patience limit and a longer button_up would have lost him. Plausible — but the 'is that fair' handshake and text follow-up promise are low-friction additions that don't require extended conversation.
coaching_implication: Three of the five required button_up elements are missing. The rep gets no credit for this booking if Andrew no-shows, and the missing elements are the ones that reduce no-show rate. In the 1-on-1: walk through the button_up checklist item by item and ask the rep to identify what they said for each one.

quote: I have I pay 2 to $100 per month. I use 13,000 kilowatt hours per year.
stewart_read: Andrew volunteers his annual kWh usage unprompted — 13,000 kWh/year is a meaningful qualification data point that the rep acknowledges ('Perfect. That that will help a lot') and immediately drops. This is usable anchor material: the rep could have reflected the number back as confirmation of system sizing potential and used it to build anticipation for the design. Instead it was filed and forgotten.
alternative_hypothesis: The rep's 'Perfect. That that will help a lot' is a brief acknowledgment before moving to the appointment ask — possibly a deliberate pace choice given Andrew's communication pattern. Plausible, but the codex's bill_anchoring principle applies to kWh data as much as dollar amounts: capture is necessary, flip is the move.
coaching_implication: Andrew handed the rep a specific, useful number and the rep said 'perfect' and moved on. In the 1-on-1: 'When he told you 13,000 kilowatt hours a year, what did that tell you about his system? Did you say anything about it?' The goal is to build the habit of reflecting qualification data back as a curiosity signal, not just logging it.

quote: What do you need to information?
stewart_read: The rep asks 'what kinda got you interested in solar?' — the codex's designated emotional anchor question — and Andrew's response is a confused non-answer. The rep's follow-up is a restatement of the question ('Just trying to get the information?') rather than a genuine attempt to understand Andrew's motivation. The interest question is the one place in the verify sequence where depth matters more than the answer, and the rep treated it as a checkbox.
alternative_hypothesis: Andrew's confusion may reflect a language barrier rather than disinterest — the rep may have correctly read that pushing deeper would create friction. Plausible, but the rep's restatement ('Just trying to get the information?') is a leading question that answers itself, not an attempt to find the real motivation.
coaching_implication: The interest question is the emotional anchor for the entire call. When Andrew couldn't answer it, the rep had two options: simplify the question ('Is it mainly about the electric bill?') or accept the non-answer and move on. The rep chose a third option — restate the question as a leading prompt — which produced no usable anchor. In the 1-on-1: 'What did you learn about why Andrew wants solar?' If the rep can't answer, that's the coaching gap.

quote: Okay. Thank you. Okay. Thank you, Audrey. You have
stewart_read: The rep closes the call by calling Andrew 'Audrey' — a name error at the final moment of the interaction. This is not a minor slip: the customer's name is the most personal element of the call, and getting it wrong at close undermines whatever rapport was built. The error also creates a practical risk — if the closer opens with 'Andrew' and Andrew remembers being called 'Audrey,' it signals a disorganized handoff.
alternative_hypothesis: The transcript may reflect a Deepgram transcription error rather than the rep actually saying 'Audrey.' Possible — but the error appears in the transcript and must be flagged until confirmed otherwise.
coaching_implication: Flag for the closer: the rep may have used the wrong name at close. Closer should open with 'Andrew' and not reference the setter call in a way that surfaces the error. In the 1-on-1 with the rep: 'What name did you use at the end of the call?' This is a basic name-discipline issue — the rep should confirm the customer's name before hanging up.
